draft.introduction: Inanna and the Me narrates the goddess Inanna’s journey to Eridu, where she obtains the divine powers (me) that govern civilization from the god Enki. The me encompass everything from kingship and truth to the art of lovemaking and the kindling of strife.

translation.original_ref: Inanna and the Me, Sumerian composition
translation.text: Enki gave to Inanna the me of lordship, the me of godship, the me of the exalted crown, the me of the throne of kingship. He gave her the me of truth, the me of descent into the underworld, the me of the art of lovemaking, the me of the kissing of the phallus, the me of prostitution, the me of forthright speech, the me of the troubled heart.
translation.original_text: [Sumerian cuneiform, tablets from Nippur, c. 2000 BCE]

translator_notes.note: The me are not merely cultural inventions but divine powers that make civilization possible. That they include both “truth” and “deceit,” both “joy” and “lament,” suggests the Sumerians understood civilization as encompassing the totality of human experience, including its darker aspects.
